utterances: That's why more than a hundred Democrats in the House and the Senate who had access to the same intelligence voted to support removing Saddam Hussein from power.

Democrats do not dispute that they saw some of the same intelligence that the president did, but they say Mr. Bush and his allies used the intelligence selectively and made presentations to Congress and to the public that exaggerated the danger posed by Iraq.
A Senate investigation found that the prewar intelligence was deeply flawed, but that the intelligence agencies were not influenced by the administration.
Democrats are now calling for an investigation of the second question: how the White House used the intelligence in making its case for war.
They say their vote to authorize war didn't mean they would support the president at every turn in Iraq.
Mr. Bush's message today was that such questions are irresponsible.
These baseless attacks send the wrong signal to our troops and to an enemy that is questioning America's will.
As our troops fight a ruthless enemy determined to destroy our way of life, they deserve to know that their elected leaders who voted to send them to war continue to stand behind them.
(Soundbite of cheering and applause)
Democrats quickly fired back.

One of them, Senator Edward Kennedy of Massachusetts, said the president was, quote, ""using Veterans Day as a campaignlike attempt to rebuild his own credibility by tearing down those who seek the truth."" Mr. Bush went after Democrats as an Associated Press poll found his job approval rating at an all-time low, 37 percent.
When respondents were asked why they didn't approve, the most frequent answer was the war in Iraq.
David Greene, NPR News, the White House.
I'm Robert Smith at the Tobyhanna Army Depot in Pennsylvania.
The president's speech delighted that assembled crowd of veterans here from World War II, Korea, Vietnam and Iraq.

While some joked that its 50-minute length was excessive, Dan Murphy, who was in the Air Force during the '80s, says that's OK because the president needed to restate his case for going to war in Iraq.
(Soundbite of music)
...been four years since 9/11, and I think we need to be reminded once in a while what we're doing over there, why we're there, why we're still there.
The president drew huge applause when he took on his critics, especially when he said that they should remain firm when the going gets tough.
John Burns from Bushkill, Pennsylvania, served in the Korean War.
He says nobody wants to shut down dissent about this war.
That's your right--of every American, to question it.
And controversy gives good results, but there's the right way and the wrong way.
My opinion with some of the senators, they turn with the flow of the weather.
You know, if you agree to do something, let's do it and let's finish it and let's do it the right way.

But Michael Bowter(ph), who just returned from Iraq duty with the military police, says that the whole reason that the US went to Iraq is to foster democracy and that means standing behind it at home.
I do think that there's a reason for question only because of the society we live in and the culture we live in.
We want to make sure that we're doing the right thing and that we're doing what's best, both for, you know, homeland, for our country, and for the international community.
Another worker here at the Army depot in the audience, Russell Barrett, says it isn't necessarily political criticism that's causing low morale.
I thought it should have been better thought out before he went in, you know, but that's hindsight.
That's me thinking after the fact.
But Barrett, who supports the president, says that you can't be too hard on the man just because of hindsight.
Pearl Harbor, our politicians, according to the records, didn't believe the intelligence they were getting, you know.
And this time he believed the intelligence and now people are trying to throw it back in his face.
And Barrett says the president shouldn't have to re-debate the reasons for the war when he's having a rough enough time already.
Robert Smith, NPR News, at the Tobyhanna Army Depot in Pennsylvania.
Today marks the official end of Israel's presence in Gaza after a 38-year occupation.
Three weeks ago, Israeli security forces removed all Jewish settlers.
Today, the army itself pulled out.
NPR's Linda Gradstein went to Gaza for what was supposed to be a ceremony handing power over to the Palestinian Authority.
She's on the line now.
Hi, Linda.
Hi, Debbie.
Farai, you're dead on.
And I mean, it's ridiculous, Farai, really.
It's 48 football fields of companies here.
I mean, 48 football fields!
You cannot really get through this whole show in the three or four days that you're allotted.
And you're right, I mean, the hype is so hysterical.

I mean, everyone from opposite ends of the spectrum--you have XM Satellite Radio pushing out new portable handheld radios that can now not only get satellite music but also record and be an MP3 player at the same time, and they brought in Donny Osmond on one end of the spectrum and then Snoop Dogg on the other end of the spectrum and Ludacris somewhere in between all of that.

So it's really amazing how you have Tom Cruise, who showed up at Yahoo!'s keynote address, along with Ellen DeGeneres--I mean, the glitz and glamour of Vegas really comes alive, but the idea is, how can you sift through all this hype to really find out what's substantive and what really is going to impact listeners and impact consumers?
Well, that's why we have you, Mario.
So what did you like?
Oh, man, there's so much.
I wish I could have packed you along so you could have come, 'cause you enjoy technology.
I really wanted to.
One of the things I think you like, because you are a wireless-only household--in other words, you use your cellular phone as your sole means for your majority of communication.
So there's a new company that has this thing called Spotwave Wireless that supposedly--I haven't tested this, but supposedly boosts your cell phone signals and helps to extend your battery coverage.

So in other words, you have a little transmitter that can sit near your window, where you get great coverage in your house, and then you have a repeater somewhere else inside the house that can repeat that cellular signal.
So you have really good coverage in spots in your house where you may not have that good coverage.
That was pretty cool.
Something else that was pretty cool--you always lose stuff, you know, especially when you're talking remote controls or keys or just--technology gets smaller and smaller.
So the locator--it's a pocket fob that pretty much lets you find anything if you remember to stick a postage-stamp-sized tag on it in advance.
That was pretty cool.
But Seiko introduced a new Spectrum watch which has a bendable display, which I thought was really neat.
Internet phoning is big, Voice over IP, and Vonage has a huge presence at this show.
And SkyScout is a product that is a handheld star-viewing assistant.
It--you kind of point it at any bright object in the sky and it will help you identify the object using global positioning system technology, which is really cool.
Well, you know, tell us a little bit more about the government's role in technology.
We've talked about that before.
Did the government show up at CES?
They did show up, and they showed up in record numbers.
This was the first year where the government actually had their own conference positioned with Consumer Electronics Show.
So there was a variety of government leaders, everyone from the chairman of FCC, Kevin Martin, all the way down to Department of Domestic Policy for President Bush.
FEMA's CIO director was here.

A lot of folks in government were here today to talk about how information can be moved, how we can move information securely, how are we going to deal with advances in health care and patient care and identity theft.
It was really kind of eye-opening that they chose this forum to have a strong government presence.
But in the end it made a lot of sense.
At first I was somewhat confused by it, but I was actually glad to see that there was this mixture of these two different spectrums coming together.

And one thing that I think we all need to keep an eye out for is the new legislation for high-definition television which basically says in 2009, if you do not have a digital television set, your TV will be snow; you'll be blacked out.
Wow.
Yes.
That's going to make for a lot of unhappy Americans.
So what's going to happen around that?
Well, see, the deal is that they're supposed to be allotting this spectrum.
They want to get rid of this analog spectrum, which is what a lot of our television sets are on now, and use that for other things like first responders, post-9/11 emergency type of issues.
They want to reallocate that spectrum.
And they can sell that spectrum and make a good penny off of it.
But on the flip side, they're pushing everyone to digital, and not every consumer is really embracing this change because this may mean costs for you.
Your current analog TV set will have to have a digital cable box connected to it in order to get over-the-air free programming.
Now if you already have cable or satellite or those types of things, you'll be fine, so the sky's not falling for you.
The sky would be falling, though, if in fact all you have is the over-the-air broadcast to your TV and maybe you still use rabbit ears and things of that nature.
You will be blacked out.

So the government is looking at, well, how do we make this transition smooth, and how could we develop a subsidy program so if low-income families or middle-income families can't afford these digital set-top boxes, they can put some type of program together to make these available to everyone so that their TVs won't go black?
Mario Armstrong covers technology for Baltimore-area NPR member stations WEAA and WYPR.
You can find his blog linked on npr.org.
And, of course, Mario is also the technology advocate for Baltimore Mayor Martin O'Malley.
Thanks, Mario.
Thank you, Farai.
That was NPR's Farai Chideya.
I'm Ed Gordon and this is NEWS & NOTES.
We turn now to Political Corner.
NPR's Juan Williams and our insiders have the latest from the nation's capital.
Juan.
Try this one: French for 17.
How well do you know French, Lydia?
Her mom died recently, her daughter's just married, and Vicente's about to retire from her job as a civil servant.
She doesn't mind sitting outside getting therapy in full view of everyone.
(Through interpreter) In an office, you're not interacting in your own environment.
Here, there are people passing by.
There are dogs, drunks, people on drugs.
For me, that's real life.
Coming to this scruffy city square on this grim day seems to have helped Vicente a lot in her battle with real life.
(Speaking Portuguese).
